rules.reference: Food and Drug Regulations (Canada) C.01.004

rules.exact_text: In the case of official drugs labelled with a brand name, the lettering of the proper name must be in type not less than half the size of the brand name and must immediately precede or follow the brand name
The brand name must be consistent on all labelling
rules.reference: Health Canada Guidance: Labelling of pharmaceutical drugs for human use, section 3.4.1

rules.exact_text: Where a package of a drug product has only one label, that label must carry all the information required on the inner and outer labels

Exception: when the narcotic present in the combination is codeine in an amount that does not require a prescription (e.g., 8 mg), the symbol "N" is required and, depending on the other ingredients, possibly a "C" symbol or the "Pr" symbol.
rules.reference: Health Canada Guidance: Labelling of pharmaceutical drugs for human use, Section 3.4.5

rules.exact_text: Where the container is too small to accommodate an inner label that conforms to the Regulations, the inner label must show the potency of the drug, except where for a drug with more than one medicinal ingredient the name used is unique for a particular potency.
rules.reference: Food and Drug Regulations C.01.004(3)(b)(ii)
Health Canada Guidance: Labelling of pharmaceutical drugs for human use, Section 5.2.2 (Directions for Use)

rules.exact_text: For professional use only.
rules.reference: Guidance document: Labelling of pharmaceutical drugs for human use, Section 5.6 (Professional Use Products)
Food and Drug Regulations (Canada) C.01.004.01(1)(a)

rules.exact_text: Proper or common names should not be abbreviated
The term "Expiration" or its abbreviation "EXP." is acceptable as a bilingual expression.
rules.reference: Health Canada Guidance: Labelling of pharmaceutical drugs for human use, Section 3.5.3

rules.exact_text: Where the container is too small to accommodate an inner label that conforms to the Regulations, the inner label must show the lot number of the drug.
rules.reference: Food and Drug Regulations C.01.004(3)(b)(v)

rules.exact_text: Where the container is too small to accommodate an inner label that conforms to the Regulations, the inner label requirements do not apply if there is an outer label that complies with the labelling requirements of the Regulations.
rules.reference: Food and Drug Regulations C.01.004(3)(a)
Guidance document: Labelling of pharmaceutical drugs for human use (Canada), Section 4.9.7
Food and Drug Regulations (Canada) C.01.004(1)(c)(iii)
Health Canada Guidance: Labelling of pharmaceutical drugs for human use, section 2.9

rules.exact_text: when a drug product has a proper or a common name, this name must appear on the main panel of the inner and outer labels
The Health Canada approved brand name must be presented in a continuous, uninterrupted fashion on the label, and be clearly evident to consumers and healthcare professionals
rules.reference: Health Canada Guidance: Labelling of pharmaceutical drugs for human use, section 3.4.1

rules.exact_text: Where a proper or common name for a drug product does not exist (e.g., for a multiple ingredient product), the pharmaceutical form should be shown on the main panel of the inner and outer label.
rules.reference: Health Canada Guidance: Labelling of pharmaceutical drugs for human use, Section 3.4.2.3

rules.exact_text: When a drug product contains a combination of ingredients from two or more schedules or ingredients listed in the Prescription Drug List, only the scheduling symbol corresponding to the more stringent control is required on the label (exception described for certain codeine combinations).
rules.reference: Health Canada Guidance: Labelling of pharmaceutical drugs for human use, Section 3.4.5

rules.exact_text: If the drug product bears a "C" or "Pr" symbol, then a prescription is still required.
rules.reference: Health Canada Guidance: Labelling of pharmaceutical drugs for human use, Section 3.4.5

rules.exact_text: Pharmacy Bulk Package (Vial) - not for direct infusion.
rules.reference: Guidance document: Labelling of pharmaceutical drugs for human use, Section 5.4.4 (Pharmacy bulk vials additional labelling)

rules.exact_text: Single puncture - multiple dispensing.
rules.reference: Guidance document: Labelling of pharmaceutical drugs for human use, Section 5.4.4 (Pharmacy bulk vials additional labelling)
Food and Drug Regulations (Canada) C.01.004 and C.01.005
Food and Drug Regulations (Canada) C.01.004
Food and Drug Regulations (Canada) C.01.004
Food and Drug Regulations (Canada) A.01.016

rules.exact_text: For different solvated forms (e.g., including hydrates), the complete common name, including the solvated form, should be stated somewhere on the label.
rules.reference: Health Canada Guidance: Labelling of pharmaceutical drugs for human use, Section 3.4.2 (solvated forms)

rules.exact_text: The appropriate scheduling symbol for certain controlled/prescription drug products or products containing an ingredient listed in the Prescription Drug List must appear in the upper left quarter of the principal display (main) panel of the label.
rules.reference: Food and Drug Regulations (Canada) C.01.004(1)(b); Health Canada Guidance Section 3.4.5
Food and Drug Regulations (cautionary statements in quotation marks must be printed verbatim)

rules.exact_text: If the declared absence of one artificial sweetener could mislead because another artificial sweetener is present, the declared absence should be accompanied by an equally prominent declaration of the actual sweetener used.
rules.reference: Guidance document: Labelling of pharmaceutical drugs for human use, Section 4.2.2 (Artificial sweeteners)

rules.exact_text: "new"
rules.reference: Guidance document: Labelling of pharmaceutical drugs for human use, Section 4.9.3 (New)
Health Canada Guidance: Labelling of pharmaceutical drugs for human use, Section 5.2.2 (Directions for Use) and 5.2.2.3 (Routes of Administration)
Food and Drug Regulations (Canada) A.01.015(2)

rules.exact_text: bilingual mock-ups of the final labels (including mock-ups of the inner and outer label and package), the package insert and the Product Monograph are required at the time of submission

The principal display panel of the inner and outer label of a drug product sold in dosage form must normally show the DIN assigned and preceded by the words "Drug Identification Number" or "Drogue identification numérique," or both, or the letters "DIN".
rules.reference: Food and Drug Regulations (Canada) C.01.005(1); Health Canada Guidance Section 3.4.6
Guidance: standards of each individual ingredient should not be declared on the main panel of a multi-ingredient drug product

rules.exact_text: For single-dose packs, if there is insufficient space, the expiry date may be omitted.
rules.reference: Health Canada Guidance: Labelling of pharmaceutical drugs for human use (Section 3.6.3.B)
Food and Drug Regulations (Canada) C.01.004(1)(c)(iii)

rules.exact_text: Where a drug product is required to be sterile by the Regulations (section C.01.065), then the notation "sterile" "(sterile)" must normally be shown on the principal display panel of the inner and outer label.
rules.reference: Food and Drug Regulations (Canada) C.01.004(1)(a)(v) and C.01.065; Health Canada Guidance Section 3.4.4

rules.exact_text: The lot number must appear on any panel of the inner and outer labels and should be preceded by the words "Lot Number" or a suitable abbreviation (i.e. Lot No., Lot, L.).
rules.reference: Food and Drug Regulations (Canada) C.01.004(1)(c)(ii); A.01.010; Health Canada Guidance Section 3.5.2

rules.exact_text: The expiration date must be on the inner and outer labels of all drug products.
rules.reference: Food and Drug Regulations (Canada) C.01.004(1)(c)(v); Health Canada Guidance Section 3.5.3

rules.exact_text: If a drug product contains flammable ingredients, the label should show a cautionary warning indicating flammable contents and appropriate directions for use (e.g., "Do not use near an open flame").
rules.reference: Food and Drug Regulations A.01.061 to A.01.063

rules.exact_text: Labels for multiple-dose packs should include, at a minimum, the potency of the drug, except where for a drug with more than one medicinal ingredient the name used is unique for a particular potency.
rules.reference: Health Canada Guidance: Labelling of pharmaceutical drugs for human use (Section 3.6.3.A)

rules.exact_text: Statements about the presence of significant adverse effects must be accorded equal prominence in the labelling material to statements about the absence of side effect; colour, contrast, position, and spacing should be considered when assessing equal prominence.
rules.reference: Guidance document: Labelling of pharmaceutical drugs for human use, Section 4.3 (Absence of Side Effects)

rules.exact_text: Where a proper or common name for a drug product does not exist (e.g., for a multiple ingredient product), the pharmaceutical form should be shown on the main panel of the inner and outer label.
rules.reference: Health Canada Guidance: Labelling of pharmaceutical drugs for human use, Section 3.4.2.3

rules.exact_text: Where a drug product is required to be sterile by the Regulations (section C.01.065), then the notation "sterile" "(sterile)" must normally be shown on the principal display panel of the inner and outer label.
rules.reference: Food and Drug Regulations (Canada) C.01.004(1)(a)(v) and C.01.065; Health Canada Guidance Section 3.4.4

rules.exact_text: Drug products packaged in a disposable metal container designed to release pressurized contents by the use of a manually operated valve must show the cautionary statements, hazard symbols, and signal words.
rules.reference: Food and Drug Regulations A.01.061 to A.01.063

rules.exact_text: Drug products intended for ingestion, inhalation, or insertion into the human body, mouthwashes, or drug products intended for ophthalmic use, must be contained in a security package if the drug product is available to the general public in an open selection area; labels on these products must draw attention to the security feature of the package.
rules.reference: Food and Drug Regulations A.01.065

rules.exact_text: Labels for multiple-dose packs should include, at a minimum, the expiry date.
rules.reference: Health Canada Guidance: Labelling of pharmaceutical drugs for human use (Section 3.6.3.A)

rules.exact_text: Health Canada recommends expressing the expiry date using patterns such as YYYYMM (with last day of month assumed) or YYYYMMDD, using two-letter month abbreviations (JA, FE, MR, AL, MA, JN, JL, AU, SE, OC, NO, DE).
rules.reference: Health Canada Guidance: Labelling of pharmaceutical drugs for human use, Section 3.5.3; Food and Drug Regulations (Canada) B.01.007
Food and Drug Regulations C.01.004(1)(c)(iii)

rules.exact_text: is to be used only on the advice of a physician
rules.reference: Food and Drug Regulations C.01.025
Guidance: transdermal patch itself should be labelled with minimum information including quantitative declaration of medicinal ingredients and delivery rate

rules.exact_text: Where the container is too small to accommodate an inner label that conforms to the Regulations, the inner label must show the route of administration if other than oral.
rules.reference: Food and Drug Regulations C.01.004(3)(b)(iv)

rules.exact_text: Where the container is too small to accommodate an inner label that conforms to the Regulations, the inner label must show the name of the manufacturer/sponsor of the drug.
rules.reference: Food and Drug Regulations C.01.004(3)(b)(vi)

rules.exact_text: "improved"
rules.reference: Guidance document: Labelling of pharmaceutical drugs for human use, Section 4.9.4 (Improved)

rules.reference: Guidance document: Labelling of pharmaceutical drugs for human use, Section 4.9.6 (Extra Strength)

rules.exact_text: a statement to the effect that any injury to a person's health that is suspected of being associated with the use of the drug may be reported to the contact person
rules.reference: Food and Drug Regulations (Canada) C.01.004.01(1)(b)

rules.exact_text: The Regulations require that the label display the name and address of the manufacturer/sponsor or manufacturers/sponsors.
rules.reference: Food and Drug Regulations (Canada) C.01.004; Health Canada Guidance Section 3.5.1

rules.exact_text: If the address shown is not Canadian, then the name of the Canadian importer and the name and address of the distributor must be added.
rules.reference: Food and Drug Regulations (Canada) C.01.004.1; Health Canada Guidance Section 3.5.1

rules.exact_text: The lot number must appear on any panel of the inner and outer labels and should be preceded by the words "Lot Number" or a suitable abbreviation (i.e. Lot No., Lot, L.).
rules.reference: Food and Drug Regulations (Canada) C.01.004(1)(c)(ii); A.01.010; Health Canada Guidance Section 3.5.2
Guidance: transdermal patch itself should be labelled with minimum information including brand name and common/proper name
Guidance: implant labelling should include total quantitative declaration per implant, mean dose delivered per unit time, and duration of use

rules.exact_text: Where the container is too small to accommodate an inner label that conforms to the Regulations, the inner label must show the proper name of the drug, the common name if there is no proper name, or for a drug with more than one medicinal ingredient, the brand name.
rules.reference: Food and Drug Regulations C.01.004(3)(b)(i)

rules.exact_text: Where the container is too small to accommodate an inner label that conforms to the Regulations, the inner label must show the expiration date of the drug.
rules.reference: Food and Drug Regulations C.01.004(3)(b)(vii)

rules.exact_text: Labels for multiple-dose packs should include, at a minimum, the lot number.
rules.reference: Health Canada Guidance: Labelling of pharmaceutical drugs for human use (Section 3.6.3.A)

rules.exact_text: The expiration date must be on the inner and outer labels of all drug products.
rules.reference: Food and Drug Regulations (Canada) C.01.004(1)(c)(v); Health Canada Guidance Section 3.5.3

rules.exact_text: The inner and outer labels must show adequate directions for use of the drug product, interpreted to include route of administration when the route is not obvious.
rules.reference: Food and Drug Regulations (Canada) C.01.004(1)(c)(iii); Health Canada Guidance Section 3.5.4

rules.exact_text: For use in pharmacy (hospital) admixture program only.
rules.reference: Guidance document: Labelling of pharmaceutical drugs for human use, Section 5.4.4 (Pharmacy bulk vials additional labelling)

rules.exact_text: The principal display panel of the inner and outer label of a drug product sold in dosage form must normally show the DIN assigned and preceded by the words "Drug Identification Number" or "Drogue identification numérique," or both, or the letters "DIN".
rules.reference: Food and Drug Regulations (Canada) C.01.005(1); Health Canada Guidance Section 3.4.6

rules.exact_text: The address should be sufficiently complete so that Canada Post could deliver a letter mailed to that address; the postal code should be included in all cases; any non-Canadian addresses that appear on the label should include the name of the country.
rules.reference: Health Canada Guidance: Labelling of pharmaceutical drugs for human use, Section 3.5.1

rules.exact_text: Labels for multiple-dose packs should include, at a minimum, the brand name or if no brand name, then the proper or common name plus the manufacturer's/sponsor's name.
rules.reference: Health Canada Guidance: Labelling of pharmaceutical drugs for human use (Section 3.6.3.A)

rules.exact_text: "complete"/"total"
rules.reference: Guidance document: Labelling of pharmaceutical drugs for human use, Section 4.9.7 (Complete, Total)

rules.exact_text: The inner and outer labels must show adequate directions for use of the drug product, interpreted to include route of administration when the route is not obvious.
rules.reference: Food and Drug Regulations (Canada) C.01.004(1)(c)(iii); Health Canada Guidance Section 3.5.4
Food and Drug Regulations C.01.004(1)(c)(iii)

rules.exact_text: If products differ only in the presence or absence of a preservative, the label should clearly identify the presence or absence of a preservative, either by reference in the brand name or directly and prominently on the label.
rules.reference: Health Canada Guidance: Labelling of pharmaceutical drugs for human use (Section 3.6)
